items.prompt_md: While finding the minimum of a rotated sorted array, you see `nums[mid] > nums[hi]`. What does that tell you?
options: The array isn't actually rotated after all
The rotation point (minimum) is to the right of mid, so `lo = mid + 1`
The rotation point (minimum) is at or to the left of mid, so `hi = mid`
items.answer: The rotation point (minimum) is to the right of mid, so `lo = mid + 1`
items.explanation_md: If `nums[mid] > nums[hi]`, the sequence from `mid` to `hi` must contain a drop — that drop is the rotation point — so the minimum lies strictly after `mid`.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array that was rotated at an unknown pivot and must find its minimum value faster than a linear scan. Which approach fits?
options: Scan the whole array once, tracking the smallest value seen
Compare the middle element against an endpoint to find which half is still sorted, then discard the other half
Sort the array again from scratch and take the first element
Keep a hash set of visited values while scanning
items.answer: Compare the middle element against an endpoint to find which half is still sorted, then discard the other half
items.explanation_md: Even though the whole array isn't sorted, one half of any split always is — comparing against an endpoint identifies that half and lets you halve the range, just like ordinary binary search.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of finding the minimum in a rotated sorted array of length n using the halving approach?
items.explanation_md: Each comparison against an endpoint discards half of the remaining range, so the search converges in about log2(n) steps.
